stem: A 14-month-old girl presents to her general practitioner with 24 hours of fever to 39 degrees Celsius, irritability and bilateral ear pulling. She is feeding less well but is interactive between febrile episodes. Her vaccinations are up to date and she has had no prior ear infections. She is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 39.0 degrees Celsius, HR 144, RR 30, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Both tympanic membranes are bulging, opaque and immobile on pneumatic otoscopy; no otorrhoea; chest clear; throat unremarkable; no neck stiffness

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Symptomatic management with regular paracetamol and review in 48 hours if not improving
options.rationale: Watchful waiting suits children over 2 years with unilateral AOM and mild illness; bilateral AOM in a child under 2 has a higher bacterial yield and antibiotics are recommended. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Oral azithromycin 10 mg/kg/dose once daily for 3 days as a single-dose option
options.rationale: Macrolides are not first-line in AOM in Australia because of pneumococcal macrolide resistance; they are reserved for confirmed non-severe penicillin allergy. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 25 mg/kg/dose three times daily for 10 days as a high-dose course
options.rationale: High-dose three-times-daily amoxicillin for 10 days reflects older North American protocols; Australian guidelines use 15 mg/kg BD for 5 days and reserve longer courses for treatment failure. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg/dose twice daily for 5 days with safety-net advice
options.rationale: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines recommend amoxicillin 15 mg/kg up to 500 mg orally twice daily for 5 days for AOM requiring antibiotics, which includes bilateral AOM under 2 years. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

options.text: Oral cefaclor 10 mg/kg/dose three times daily for 5 days as a cephalosporin alternative
options.rationale: Cefaclor has inferior pharmacokinetics against pneumococcus compared with amoxicillin and is reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy when amoxicillin cannot be used. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Acute otitis media)

explanation.summary: Bilateral acute otitis media in a child under 2 years is an indication for antibiotic therapy in Australian guidelines. Amoxicillin 15 mg/kg up to 500 mg twice daily for 5 days is first-line. Watchful waiting suits children over 2 years with unilateral AOM and mild illness. Macrolides and oral cephalosporins are reserved for penicillin allergy.
key_points: Amoxicillin 15 mg/kg BD for 5 days is the AU first-line for AOM requiring antibiotics
Bilateral AOM under 2 years is an indication for antibiotics rather than observation
Macrolides are reserved for non-severe penicillin allergy due to pneumococcal resistance
explanation.pearls: Watchful waiting applies to children over 2 years with unilateral non-severe AOM; bilateral disease, age under 2, otorrhoea, or Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander identity shifts the decision to antibiotics.

stem: A 4-year-old boy presents to a metropolitan GP with 3 weeks of dry cough, now with paroxysms of coughing followed by an inspiratory whoop and post-tussive vomiting. He is otherwise well between episodes. His mother is 32 weeks pregnant with her second child. A 6-week-old cousin lives in the same house. The child had three doses of pertussis-containing vaccine in infancy. NAAT swab returns positive for Bordetella pertussis.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius, HR 96, RR 24, SpO2 98% in room air
data_table.Examination: Well between paroxysms; chest clear; no respiratory distress at rest

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step for the index child and the household?

options.text: Treat the index child with azithromycin and offer prophylaxis to all household contacts regardless of risk
options.rationale: Australian post-exposure prophylaxis is targeted to high-risk contacts (third-trimester pregnancy, infants under 6 months, household contacts of these vulnerable people) rather than the entire household. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis)

options.text: Symptomatic management only as the index child is past the contagious period and no longer infectious
options.rationale: Pertussis treatment within 21 days of cough onset shortens infectivity and is still warranted at 3 weeks; transmission to vulnerable contacts in the household remains a concern. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Pertussis)

options.text: Treat the index child with amoxicillin and offer the same antibiotic to all household contacts
options.rationale: Amoxicillin is not active against Bordetella pertussis; the first-line agent for treatment and prophylaxis is azithromycin (or clarithromycin or trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole if macrolide intolerant). (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Pertussis)

options.text: Treat the index child with azithromycin and provide household prophylaxis to high-risk contacts
options.rationale: Australian Therapeutic Guidelines recommend azithromycin for confirmed pertussis within 21 days of cough onset, and post-exposure prophylaxis with azithromycin for high-risk contacts including pregnant women in the third trimester and infants under 6 months. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Pertussis)

options.text: Defer treatment and arrange paired pertussis serology before deciding on antibiotic prophylaxis
options.rationale: NAAT is the confirmatory test and serology is no longer recommended in this clinical context; treatment and contact tracing should not be delayed. (Source: CDNA SoNG Pertussis)

explanation.summary: Pertussis is treated with azithromycin if started within 21 days of cough onset, primarily to reduce onward transmission. Australian guidelines target post-exposure prophylaxis to high-risk contacts: third-trimester pregnant women, infants under 6 months, and household contacts of these vulnerable people. Amoxicillin is ineffective; macrolides are first-line.
key_points: Azithromycin treats pertussis within 21 days of cough onset and reduces transmission
Prophylaxis is targeted to third-trimester pregnant contacts and infants under 6 months
Amoxicillin and beta-lactams have no role in pertussis
explanation.pearls: The pregnant mother is herself eligible for pertussis vaccination in pregnancy (20-32 weeks) and the 6-week-old cousin is the most vulnerable contact; both require attention beyond chemoprophylaxis.

stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to a GP because his parents are worried he looks pale and has been a fussy eater. He drinks 800 mL of cow's milk daily and refuses red meat. He met motor milestones on time and is now running. There is no family history of haemoglobinopathy and he is not Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. Recent FBC and iron studies are shown.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius, HR 124, RR 24, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Pale conjunctivae; no jaundice; no hepatosplenomegaly; no lymphadenopathy; no rash
data_table.Investigations: Hb 84 g/L, MCV 62 fL, MCH 19 pg, reticulocytes 0.6%, ferritin 6 microgram/L, transferrin saturation 5%, CRP 2 mg/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Arrange urgent haematology referral for bone marrow biopsy to exclude marrow failure as the cause
options.rationale: The microcytic, hypochromic picture with very low ferritin and saturation is diagnostic of iron deficiency; bone marrow biopsy is not warranted before a trial of oral iron with reassessment. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Commence oral ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg of elemental iron once daily and reduce cow's milk to 500 mL/day
options.rationale: Australian paediatric guidance for iron deficiency anaemia is elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg/day orally, and reducing cow's milk to under 500 mL/day removes the dietary driver. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Request haemoglobin electrophoresis and defer treatment until thalassaemia trait has been excluded
options.rationale: Ferritin 6 microgram/L confirms iron deficiency; electrophoresis is reserved for atypical patterns or persistent microcytosis after iron repletion. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Arrange an intravenous iron infusion in day surgery because oral iron is poorly tolerated in toddlers
options.rationale: IV iron in toddlers is reserved for failure or intolerance of oral therapy or malabsorption; a trial of oral iron with dietary counselling is the first step. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

options.text: Commence vitamin B12 and folate supplementation as the most likely cause of nutritional anaemia
options.rationale: The MCV is low rather than high, making B12 or folate deficiency unlikely; the iron studies confirm iron deficiency as the diagnosis. (Source: RCH CPG: Iron deficiency)

explanation.summary: Microcytic anaemia with very low ferritin and transferrin saturation in a toddler with excess cow's milk intake is iron deficiency anaemia from a dietary cause. Oral elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg/day with reduction of cow's milk to under 500 mL/day is first-line. FBC is repeated at 2 to 4 weeks; ongoing iron is continued for 3 months after Hb normalises to replenish stores.
key_points: Elemental iron 3 to 6 mg/kg/day orally is first-line in paediatric iron deficiency
Cow's milk above 500 mL/day in toddlers is a major risk factor and must be reduced
Continue oral iron for 3 months after Hb normalises to replenish ferritin stores
explanation.pearls: A reticulocyte rise at day 7 to 10 and a 10 to 20 g/L rise in Hb at 4 weeks confirms response; failure prompts adherence check, dose review, malabsorption work-up, then IV iron.

stem: A 4-year-old girl presents to a GP with 6 months of hard, painful stools every 4 to 5 days, often with streaks of fresh blood and occasional faecal soiling between bowel actions. She has been holding stools at preschool. There is no abdominal mass on examination but a palpable rectal faecal mass is felt suprapubically. Growth is on the 50th centile. There are no red flag features for organic disease.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 36.6 degrees Celsius, HR 92, RR 22, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, palpable suprapubic faecal mass, no organomegaly; perianal inspection shows a small posterior anal fissure; anal tone normal; spine and lower limb neurology normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer urgently for rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung disease as the cause of chronic constipation
options.rationale: Hirschsprung disease presents earlier (often delayed meconium and failure to thrive); a 4-year-old with normal growth, soiling and a fissure has functional constipation. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Administer a phosphate enema in the clinic then commence lactulose for maintenance therapy at home
options.rationale: Oral macrogol is preferred over rectal disimpaction in children because it is less distressing and equally effective; phosphate enemas risk hyperphosphataemia in small children. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Arrange abdominal X-ray to quantify faecal loading and base disimpaction decisions on the radiographic score
options.rationale: Plain abdominal X-ray is not recommended in routine functional constipation; clinical assessment guides therapy and X-ray adds radiation without altering management. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Commence oral macrogol 3350 with electrolytes at a disimpaction dose for up to 7 days then transition to maintenance
options.rationale: Australian paediatric guidance favours oral macrogol 3350 with electrolytes for both disimpaction (escalating dose for up to 7 days) and maintenance in functional constipation, with ongoing behavioural toileting. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

options.text: Recommend dietary fibre and increased fluid intake alone for 4 weeks before considering laxative therapy
options.rationale: Dietary advice alone rarely clears impaction and prolongs symptoms; macrogol with electrolytes is first-line alongside behavioural and dietary measures. (Source: RCH CPG: Constipation)

explanation.summary: Functional constipation with faecal impaction in a preschool-aged child is treated with oral macrogol 3350 with electrolytes at a disimpaction dose for up to 7 days, then maintenance dose for several months alongside toilet sit routines and behavioural interventions. Rectal therapies are reserved for failed oral disimpaction. Imaging is rarely required.
key_points: Oral macrogol with electrolytes is first-line for both disimpaction and maintenance
Maintenance is continued for months past resolution to avoid relapse and re-impaction
Red flags (delayed meconium, faltering growth, perianal disease) prompt secondary review
explanation.pearls: Anal fissures from passing hard stools maintain the withholding cycle; topical local anaesthetic and stool softening together break the loop.

sources.label: NICE NG: Constipation in children and young people

stem: A 7-year-old boy presents to ED with a 5-day history of palpable purpuric rash over the buttocks and lower limbs, intermittent crampy abdominal pain and bilateral ankle pain. Today he has had two episodes of dark cola-coloured urine. He had a viral upper respiratory illness 2 weeks ago. His mother says urine output is reduced. Investigations are shown.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 37.1 degrees Celsius, HR 104, RR 22, BP 132/88 mmHg, SpO2 99% in room air
data_table.Examination: Palpable purpura over buttocks and lower legs, abdomen soft with mild tenderness, no peritonism, ankles swollen but full range, no neurological deficit
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 3+ blood, 2+ protein with red cell casts; urine protein-creatinine ratio 180 mg/mmol; creatinine 78 micromol/L; urea 9 mmol/L; platelets 380 x10^9/L; coags normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate disposition and initial management?

options.text: Discharge with paracetamol and outpatient urinalysis weekly because skin and joint signs predominate
options.rationale: Hypertension and nephritic-nephrotic urinary findings exceed simple HSP and mandate admission; outpatient management is reserved for isolated rash with normal BP and urinalysis. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schoenlein purpura)

options.text: Admit for blood pressure control, monitoring of renal function and paediatric nephrology consultation
options.rationale: Macroscopic haematuria with hypertension and nephrotic-range proteinuria in HSP signals significant nephritis requiring inpatient monitoring, antihypertensive therapy, and early nephrology input. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schoenlein purpura)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg in the emergency department and discharge with follow-up in a week
options.rationale: Steroids may help abdominal pain or arthritis but do not prevent or treat established nephritis; severe nephritis requires admission, BP control, and nephrology input. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schoenlein purpura)

options.text: Request abdominal ultrasound to exclude intussusception then discharge if no lead point is identified
options.rationale: Intussusception screening is reasonable with severe abdominal pain in HSP but does not address the dominant problem of nephritis with hypertension. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schoenlein purpura)

options.text: Commence empirical broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics for suspected post-infectious glomerulonephritis
options.rationale: The rash, joint involvement, abdominal pain and timing make HSP nephritis the unifying diagnosis; broad-spectrum antibiotics are not indicated in the absence of sepsis. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schoenlein purpura)

explanation.summary: Henoch-Schoenlein purpura with macroscopic haematuria, hypertension and nephrotic-range proteinuria represents severe nephritis. Australian practice is admission for blood pressure control, serial renal function and urinalysis, and early paediatric nephrology consultation. Children with isolated rash and arthralgia, normal BP and bland urine can be managed as outpatients with weekly urinalysis for 6 months.
key_points: BP above the 95th centile with red-cell casts in HSP warrants admission
Steroids treat pain and arthritis but do not prevent or modify HSP nephritis
All HSP children need 6 months of urinalysis and BP monitoring to detect late nephritis
explanation.pearls: Renal biopsy is considered when nephrotic-range proteinuria persists beyond 4 weeks or creatinine rises; ACE inhibitor therapy is started for persistent proteinuria above 100 mg/mmol creatinine ratio.
